{{Short description|Research program in hearing sciences}}
{{Multiple issues|{{technical|date=May 2025}}
{{Citation style|date=October 2024}}
{{too many sections|date=May 2025}}
{{page numbers needed|date=July 2025}}}}
'''Human auditory ecology''' (HAE) is a research program in [[Audiology|hearing sciences]] studying the interactions between humans and their [[acoustic environment]]s.

== Concept ==
HAE studies the "relationship between the acoustic environments in which people live and their auditory needs in these environments.<ref name=":0">Gatehouse S., Elberling C., Naylor G. (1999) ''Aspects of auditory ecology and psychoacoustic function as determinants of benefits from and candidature for non-linear processing in hearing aids''. In A. N. Rasmussen, P. A. Osterhammel, T. Anderson, & T. Poulsen (Eds.), ''Auditory models and non-linear hearing instruments (18th Danavox Symposium)'' (pp. 221–233). Copenhagen, Denmark: Holmens Trykkeri</ref> This ''auditory ecology'', a concept initially coined by Stuart Gatehouse,<ref name=":0" /> therefore refers to the auditory environments in which humans live and function, the tasks to be undertaken by humans in these complex acoustic environments and the importance of these tasks in daily life and daily routines. The use of this concept was initially restricted to the case of urban life.<ref>{{cite journal | last1=Keidser | first1=Gitte | last2=Naylor | first2=Graham | last3=Brungart | first3=Douglas S. | last4=Caduff | first4=Andreas | last5=Campos | first5=Jennifer | last6=Carlile | first6=Simon | last7=Carpenter | first7=Mark G. | last8=Grimm | first8=Giso | last9=Hohmann | first9=Volker | last10=Holube | first10=Inga | last11=Launer | first11=Stefan | last12=Lunner | first12=Thomas | last13=Mehra | first13=Ravish | last14=Rapport | first14=Frances | last15=Slaney | first15=Malcolm | last16=Smeds | first16=Karolina | title=Comment on the Point of View "Ecological Validity, External Validity and Mundane Realism in Hearing Science" | journal=Ear & Hearing | date=2022 | volume=43 | issue=5 | pages=1601–1602 | doi=10.1097/AUD.0000000000001241 | pmid=35666537 }}</ref> However, urban habitats are relatively recent in humankind history and evolution, and natural soundscapes have preceded the apparition of ''Homo sapiens'', some 300,000 years ago. For this reason, the concept of ''auditory ecology'' was extended to study a different and evolutionary-based question, namely how humans perceive ecological processes at work in natural habitats through their [[Auditory system|peripheral]] and [[Auditory system|central auditory system]].

== Auditory perception of natural soundscapes ==
[[Soundscape ecology|Natural soundscapes]] correspond to the complex arrangements of biological (animal vocalizations) and [[Geophysics|geophysical]] (wind, rain, stream) sounds shaped by sound propagation through non-anthropogenic habitats.<ref name=":1">{{cite journal | last1 = Grinfeder | first1 = E. | last2 = Lorenzi | first2 = C. | last3 = Haupert | first3 = S. | last4 = Sueur | first4 = J. | year = 2022 | title = What do we mean by "soundscape"? A functional description | url = | journal = Frontiers in Ecology and Evolution | volume = 10 | issue = | article-number = 894232 | doi = 10.3389/fevo.2022.894232 | doi-access = free }}</ref><ref name=":9">Farina A., Gage S.H. (2017) Ecoacoustics: The ecological role of sounds (John Wiley & Sons, Hoboken). ISBN 978-1-119-23069-4</ref><ref name=":3">{{Cite book |last=C. Pijanowski |first=Bryan |title=Principles of Soundscape Ecology Discovering Our Sonic World |date=2024 |publisher=The University of Chicago Press }}</ref><ref>[[Acoustic ecology]]</ref> According to its most recent definition, human auditory ecology (HAE) is a multidisciplinary research programme attempting to map and explain the ability of [[Hearing|normal-hearing]] and [[Hearing loss|hearing-impaired]] human listeners to perceive natural soundscapes.<ref name=":4">{{cite journal | last1 = Fay | first1 = R | year = 2009 | title = Soundscapes and the sense of hearing of fishes | url = | journal = Integrative Zoology | volume = 4 | issue = 1| pages = 26–32 | doi = 10.1111/j.1749-4877.2008.00132.x | pmid = 21392274 }}</ref><ref name=":5">{{cite journal | last1 = Lorenzi | first1 = C. | last2 = Apoux | first2 = F. | last3 = Grinfeder | first3 = E. | last4 = Krause | first4 = B. | last5 = Miller-Viaca | first5 = N. | last6 = Sueur | first6 = J. | year = 2023 | title = Human auditory ecology: Extending hearing research to the perception of natural soundscapes by humans in rapidly-changing environments | url = | journal = Trends in Hearing | volume = 27 | issue = | pages = 1–28 | article-number = 23312165231212032 | doi = 10.1177/23312165231212032 | pmid = 37981813 | pmc = 10658775 }}</ref><ref name=":6">{{cite journal | last1 = Thoret | first1 = E. | last2 = Varnet | first2 = L. | last3 = Boubenec | first3 = Y. | last4 = Ferriere | first4 = R. | last5 = Le Tourneau | first5 = F.-M. | last6 = Krause | first6 = B. | last7 = Lorenzi | first7 = C. | year = 2020 | title = Characterizing amplitude and frequency modulation cues in natural soundscapes: A pilot study in four habitats of a biosphere reserve | url = | journal = Journal of the Acoustical Society of America | volume = 147 | issue = 5| pages = 3260–3274 | doi = 10.1121/10.0001174 | pmid = 32486802 | bibcode = 2020ASAJ..147.3260T }}</ref> In this specific case, HAE aims at characterizing how and to which extent humans perceive ecological processes underlying habitats marginally affected by human activity through their ears and their auditory brain, with a life-span perspective.<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":5" /><ref name=":6" /> HAE could be considered as a field of auditory [[psychophysics]], [[Auditory science|auditory neurosciences]] and [[audiology]]. More broadly, HAE aims to encourage hearing scientists who traditionally work on speech and music perception in urban settings to collaborate with [[Soundscape ecology#:~:text=Soundscape ecology is the study,organisms are marine or terrestrial|soundscape ecologists]], [[Bioacoustics|ecoacousticians and neuro-ethologists]], and share expertise with environmental and [[Architectural acoustics|architectural acousticians]], anthropologists, philosophers and geographers.<ref name=":5" /><ref name=":6" /><ref name=":3" />

== Extended definition of human auditory ecology ==
HAE studies the (presumably ancestral) monitoring functions of the human auditory system. These monitory auditory functions are used by human auditory system to build a perceptual representation of the close environment, orient and navigate, assess resources (food, water, shelter) and danger (e.g., flooding, predators), opportunities for action, and the general health of the environment. These monitoring functions are assumed to help human listeners build a sense of place and time and operate in their acoustic environment.<ref name=":4" /><ref name=":5" /><ref name=":0" />

From such a perspective, HAE is based on (i) concepts derived from soundscape ecology such as the acoutic adaptation and acoustic niche hypothesis,<ref name=":7">{{cite journal | last1 = Pijanowski | first1 = B.C. | last2 = Villanueva-Rivera | first2 = L.J. | last3 = Dumyahn | first3 = S.L. | last4 = Farina | first4 = A. | last5 = Krause | first5 = B.L. | last6 = Napoletano | first6 = B.M. | last7 = Gage | first7 = S.H. | last8 = Pieretti | first8 = N. | year = 2011 | title = Soundscape ecology: the science of sound in the landscape | url = | journal = BioScience | volume = 61 | issue = 3| pages = 203–216 | doi = 10.1525/bio.2011.61.3.6 | bibcode = 2011BiSci..61..203P }}</ref><ref name=":8">Sueur, J., Farina, A. Ecoacoustics: the Ecological Investigation and Interpretation of Environmental Sound. ''Biosemiotics'' '''8''', 493–502 (2015). https://doi.org/10.1007/s12304-015-9248-x</ref><ref name=":3" /><ref name=":9"/> and (ii) psychophysical and neuroscientific models and methods. HAE operates on the large acoustic databases of natural soundscapes collected by soundscape ecologists and eco-acousticians using standardized procedures and recording material.<ref name=":7" /><ref name=":8" /><ref name=":9" /> HAE aims to characterize the monitoring functions of the human auditory system through ordinary listening behaviors (listening to animal vocalisations such as bird songs or insect stridulations, detecting presence of water or rain, assessing water discharge or wind strength ...). Moreover, HAE investigates the extent to which these monitoring functions are adapted to specific information conveyed by natural soundscapes, whether they operate throughout the life span or whether they emerge through individual learning or cultural transmission.<ref name=":5" />

HAE aims to identify testable working hypotheses guided by computational models of the human auditory system, in order to (i) unveil low and high-level auditory mechanisms engaged in the auditory perception of soundscapes associated with natural habitats, green or blue species within or outside cities,<ref name=":10">{{cite journal | last1 = Thoret | year = 2020 | title = Characterizing amplitude and frequency modulation cues in natural soundscapes: A pilot study on four habitats of a biosphere reserve | url = | journal =  The Journal of the Acoustical Society of America| volume = 147 | issue = 5| pages = 3260–3274 | doi = 10.1121/10.0001174| pmid = 32486802| bibcode = 2020ASAJ..147.3260T}}</ref><ref name=":11">{{cite journal | last1 = Apoux | first1 = F. | last2 = Miller-Viacava | first2 = N. | last3 = Férriere | first3 = R. | last4 = Dai | first4 = H. | last5 = Krause | first5 = B. | last6 = Sueur | first6 = J. | last7 = Lorenzi | first7 = C. | year = 2023 | title = Auditory discrimination of natural soundscapes | url = | journal = Journal of the Acoustical Society of America | volume = 153 | issue = 5| pages = 2706–2723 | doi = 10.1121/10.0017972 | pmid = 37133815 | bibcode = 2023ASAJ..153.2706A | hdl = 10150/674530 | hdl-access = free }}</ref> (ii) how they develop through life and (iii) the extent to which they are affected by exposure, learning and culture.<ref name=":12">{{cite journal | last1 = Gervain | first1 = J. | last2 = Werker | first2 = J. F. | last3 = Geffen | first3 = M. N. | year = 2014 | title = Category-Specific Processing of Scale-Invariant Sounds in Infancy | url = | journal = PLOS ONE | volume = 9 | issue = 5| article-number = e96278 | doi = 10.1371/journal.pone.0096278 | pmid = 24809801 | pmc = 4014493 | bibcode = 2014PLoSO...996278G | doi-access = free }}</ref> Although HAE aims to improve fundamental knowledge on the human auditory system and how humans interact with natural environments, it also aims at providing novel solutions to screen and rehabilitate hearing loss via hearing aids and cochlear implants.<ref name=":13">{{cite journal | last1 = Miller-Viacava | first1 = Nicole | last2 = Lazard | first2 = Diane | last3 = Tanguy | last4 = Krause | first4 = Bernie | year = 2024| title = Sensorineural hearing loss alters auditory discrimination of natural soundscapes | url = https://hal.science/hal-04695409| journal = International Journal of Audiology | volume = 2023 | issue = 10| pages = 809–818 | doi = 10.1080/14992027.2023.2272559 | pmid = 37909429 }}</ref>

== Acoustical aspects ==

=== Contribution of soundscape ecology and ecoacoustics ===
Because most vertebrate species send and receive sound for essential life functions (e.g., navigation, courtship, foraging<ref name=":14">{{cite journal | last1 = Fay | first1 = R.R. | last2 = Popper | first2 = A.N. | title = Evolution of hearing in vertebrates: the inner ears and processing | url = | journal =Hearing Research |year=2000 | volume = 149 | issue = 1–2| pages = 1–10 | doi = 10.1016/S0378-5955(00)00168-4 | pmid = 11033242 }}</ref>), the collection of sounds perceived in an environment (i.e., soundscapes<ref>Schafer, R.M., ''The tuning of the world''. 1977, New York, NY, USA: Knopf</ref>) can reflect ecological processes.<ref name=":7" /> Thus, by studying the structure of the soundscape over space and time, soundscape ecology and ecoacoustics aim to study the dynamics of ecological processes.<ref name=":9" /><ref>{{cite journal | last1=Pijanowski | first1=Bryan C. | last2=Farina | first2=Almo | last3=Gage | first3=Stuart H. | last4=Dumyahn | first4=Sarah L. | last5=Krause | first5=Bernie L. | title=What is soundscape ecology? An introduction and overview of an emerging new science | journal=Landscape Ecology | date=2011 | volume=26 | issue=9 | pages=1213–1232 | doi=10.1007/s10980-011-9600-8 | bibcode=2011LaEco..26.1213P }}</ref> Soundscape ecology is predicated on two key hypotheses: [[Soundscape ecology|the acoustic niche hypothesis]], where signals have evolved to partition in acoustic space to minimize overlap between species<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Krause | first1 = B | title = The niche hypothesis | url = | journal =The Soundscape Newsletter |year=1993 | volume = 6 | issue = | pages = 6–10 }}</ref> and the [[Soundscape ecology|acoustic adaptation hypothesis]], which states that species' optimize transmission of vocalizations to overcome habitat constraints. Although, the acoustic adaptation hypothesis has received limited support in experimental studies.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Hardt | first1 = B. | last2 = Benedict | first2 = L. | title = Can you hear me now? A review of signal transmission and experimental evidence for the acoustic adaptation hypothesis | url = | journal =Bioacoustics |year=2021 | volume = 30 | issue = 6| pages = 716–742 | doi = 10.1080/09524622.2020.1858448 | bibcode = 2021Bioac..30..716H }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Gillard | first1 = G.L. | last2 = Rowley | first2 = J.J.L. | title = Assessment of the acoustic adaptation hypothesis in frogs using large-scale citizen science data | url = | journal =Journal of Zoology |year=2023 | volume = 320 | issue = 4| pages = 271–281 | doi = 10.1111/jzo.13088 | doi-access = free }}</ref> Recent developments in passive sound collection offer the opportunity to cost-effectively monitor soundscapes at enormous scales.<ref>{{cite journal | last1=Hill | first1=Andrew P. | last2=Prince | first2=Peter | last3=Piña Covarrubias | first3=Evelyn | last4=Doncaster | first4=C. Patrick | last5=Snaddon | first5=Jake L. | last6=Rogers | first6=Alex | last7=Isaac | first7=Nick | title=AudioMoth: Evaluation of a smart open acoustic device for monitoring biodiversity and the environment | journal=Methods in Ecology and Evolution | date=2018 | volume=9 | issue=5 | pages=1199–1211 | doi=10.1111/2041-210X.12955 | bibcode=2018MEcEv...9.1199H | doi-access=free }}</ref> For example, the Australian Acoustic Observatory comprises 360 permanent recording stations<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Roe | first1 = P. | display-authors = etal | title = The Australian Acoustic Observatory | url = | journal =Methods in Ecology and Evolution |year=2021 | volume = 12 | issue = 10| pages = 1802–1808 | doi = 10.1111/2041-210X.13660 | bibcode = 2021MEcEv..12.1802R }}</ref> and the [[National Park Service]]'s Natural Sounds and Night Skies division have collected recordings at over 490 sites across the United States.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Buxton | first1 = R.T. | display-authors = etal | title = Noise pollution is pervasive in U.S. protected areas | url = | journal =Science |year=2017 | volume = 356 | issue = 6337| pages = 531–533 | doi = 10.1126/science.aah4783 | pmid = 28473587 | bibcode = 2017Sci...356..531B }}</ref> Extracting relevant biological information from resulting enormous datasets remains challenging. &nbsp;Species vocalizations of interest may be manually or automatically extracted, using listening, visualizations of spectrograms, or recognition algorithms.<ref>{{cite journal | last1=Kahl | first1=Stefan | last2=Wood | first2=Connor M. | last3=Eibl | first3=Maximilian | last4=Klinck | first4=Holger | title=BirdNET: A deep learning solution for avian diversity monitoring | journal=Ecological Informatics | date=2021 | volume=61 | article-number=101236 | doi=10.1016/j.ecoinf.2021.101236 | bibcode=2021EcInf..6101236K | doi-access=free }}</ref> Alternatively, acoustic indices can be used to summarize the properties of the soundscape.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Bradfer-Lawrence | first1 = T. | display-authors = etal | title = Guidelines for the use of acoustic indices in environmental research | url = https://durham-repository.worktribe.com/output/1289808| journal =Methods in Ecology and Evolution |year=2019 | volume = 10 | issue = 10| pages = 1796–1807 | doi = 10.1111/2041-210X.13254 | bibcode = 2019MEcEv..10.1796B }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | last1=Buxton | first1=Rachel T. | last2=McKenna | first2=Megan F. | last3=Clapp | first3=Mary | last4=Meyer | first4=Erik | last5=Stabenau | first5=Erik | last6=Angeloni | first6=Lisa M. | last7=Crooks | first7=Kevin | last8=Wittemyer | first8=George | title=Efficacy of extracting indices from large-scale acoustic recordings to monitor biodiversity | journal=Conservation Biology | date=2018 | volume=32 | issue=5 | pages=1174–1184 | doi=10.1111/cobi.13119 | pmid=29676813 | bibcode=2018ConBi..32.1174B | doi-access=free }}</ref>

=== Contribution of computational auditory sciences ===
The pioneering work of Singh and Theunissen<ref name=":15">{{cite journal | last1 = Singh | first1 = N.C. | last2 = Theunissen | first2 = F.E. | year = 2003 | title = Modulation spectra of natural sounds and ethological theories of auditory processing | url = | journal = Journal of the Acoustical Society of America | volume = 114 | issue = 6| pages = 3394–3411 | doi = 10.1121/1.1624067 | pmid = 14714819 | bibcode = 2003ASAJ..114.3394S }}</ref> suggests that high spectral modulations (harmonicity) and slow amplitude modulations distinguish biological (animal vocalizations) from geophysical sounds (wind, rain, stream sounds) in natural scenes. Subsequent work by McDermott and Simoncelli<ref name=":16">{{cite journal | last1 = McDermott | first1 = J.H. | last2 = Simoncelli | first2 = E.P. | year = 2011 | title = Sound texture perception via statistics of the auditory periphery: evidence from sound synthesis | url = | journal = Neuron | volume = 71 | issue = 5| pages = 926–940 | doi = 10.1016/j.neuron.2011.06.032 | pmid = 21903084 | pmc = 4143345 }}</ref> showed that geophysical sounds such as wind, rain or stream sounds can be distinguished from other sounds by their textural properties. The latter are reflected in specific regularities or "statistics" of amplitude-modulation patterns computed by the human auditory system in response to these sounds.<ref name=":16" />

The statistics of these sounds and scenes could be characterized further using the large, ecologically valid databases collected by soundscape ecologists and ecoacousticians, allowing to test further efficient-coding principles positing that perceptual systems (e.g., the auditory system) have evolved to encode environmental stimuli in the most efficient way, and that the properties of auditory mechanisms &nbsp;closely match the statistical properties of natural sounds and scenes.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Lesica | first1 = N.A. | last2 = Grothe | first2 = B. | year = 2008 | title = Efficient temporal processing of naturalistic sounds | url = | journal = PLOS ONE | volume = 3 | issue = 2| article-number = e1655 | doi = 10.1371/journal.pone.0001655 | pmid = 18301738 | pmc = 2249929 | bibcode = 2008PLoSO...3.1655L | doi-access = free }}</ref><ref name=":15" /><ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Lewicki | first1 = M.S. | year = 2002 | title = Efficient coding of natural sounds | url = | journal = Nature Neuroscience | volume = 5 | issue = 4| pages = 356–363 | doi = 10.1038/nn831 | pmid = 11896400 }}</ref><ref>Attias H., Schreiner C. E. (1997) Temporal low-order statistics of natural sounds. Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems 9 : 27–33. MIT Press</ref><ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Nelken | first1 = I. | last2 = Rotman | first2 = Y. | last3 = Bar Yosef | first3 = O. | year = 1999 | title = Responses of auditory-cortex neurons to structural features of natural sounds | url = | journal = Nature | volume = 397 | issue = 6715| pages = 154–157 | doi = 10.1038/16456 | pmid = 9923676 | bibcode = 1999Natur.397..154N }}</ref> The first studies based on this approach indicate that the central auditory system of humans has access to sufficient sensory information in the spectro-temporal domain to achieve accurate auditory discrimination of terrestrial biomes and their changes across moments of the day and seasons.<ref name=":11" /><ref name=":6" />

== Psychoacoustical aspects ==

=== Historical background ===
HAE aims to understand human auditory perception of natural (i.e. biophonic and geophonic) acoustic environments. &nbsp;In contrast, the majority of experimental studies of human auditory perception have utilized parametrically generated stimuli which lack acoustic complexity of natural environmental sounds.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Schutz | first1 = M. | last2 = Gillard | first2 = J. | year = 2020 | title = On the generalization of tones: A detailed exploration of non-speech auditory perception stimuli | journal = Sci Rep | volume = 10 | issue = 1| article-number = 9520 | doi = 10.1038/s41598-020-63132-2 | pmid = 32533008 | pmc = 7293323 | bibcode = 2020NatSR..10.9520S }}</ref> The prevailing methodological paradigms of [[psychoacoustics]] have traditionally focused on investigating how sounds' physical properties relate to the perception of abstract sound qualities such as pitch, loudness and duration without considering sounds' semantic or referential aspects. Furthermore, traditional psychoacoustic methods have relied primarily on detection and discrimination tasks in which a specific acoustic parameter is manipulated (e.g. frequency or intensity) under conditions of low stimulus uncertainty and performed by trained listeners. Overall, traditional psychoacoustics has been highly successful in describing human auditory abilities in relationship to underlying anatomy and physiology, leading to tremendous breakthroughs and achievements in communication and audio technology.<ref>Plomp, R. (2002). The intelligent ear: On the nature of sound perception. Lawrence Erlbaum Associates Publishers</ref> However, this approach has had limited utility for understanding the perception of meaningful acoustically complex environmental sounds in everyday life, which typically involves perception of sound producing objects and events along with associated materials and actions.<ref name=":17">{{cite journal | last1 = Gaver | first1 = W. W. | year = 1993 | title = What in the world do we hear? An ecological approach to auditory event perception | journal = Ecological Psychology | volume = 5 | issue = 1| pages = 1–29 | doi = 10.1207/s15326969eco0501_1 }}</ref><ref>Neuhoff JG. Ecological Psychoacoustics. 1st ed. New York, NY: Elsevier Academic Press; 2004</ref>

=== Ecological psychoacoustics ===
A growing awareness of these limitations to ecological validity in traditional psychoacoustics has led to the broadening of theoretical approaches and modifications of experimental procedures used in the studies of auditory perception to include tasks involving sound identification, categorization and comprehension. &nbsp;Later efforts explored principles of perceptual organization of complex auditory scenes (under the general framework of [[auditory scene analysis]]) and formulated questions in terms of actionable and behaviorally relevant properties of specific sound (under the framework of [[ecological psychology]]). &nbsp;A further attempt to bridge the gap between investigations of auditory perception utilizing traditional psychoacoustic paradigms and those pertaining to everyday listening (ordinary listening behaviors) has been proposed under the general heading of ecological psychoacoustics. Ecological psychoacoustics generally considers auditory perception in terms of the ecologically relevant behavioral goals of the listener in specific tasks, situational contexts and environments (e.g. cognition-perception-action loop), while employing psychoacoustic experimental methods to maintain high [[internal validity]].

For example, numerous studies have investigated aspects of listeners' perception of sound producing objects, materials, and actions solely based on associated environmental sounds.<ref name=":18">Shafiro, V (2022). Environmental Sound Perception: Effects of Aging and Hearing Loss. In Encyclopedia of Computational Neuroscience, Springer New York</ref> These studies consistently demonstrate remarkably accurate perception of sound sources based on acoustic signals alone. For instance, listeners can accurately judge the size and behavior of objects, such as predicting the timing of successive bounces of different types of balls, dropped from different heights, based on preceding sounds. Additionally, listeners can discern the sex and posture of a walker from the sound of their footsteps,<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Pastore | first1 = RE | last2 = Flint | first2 = JD | last3 = Gaston | first3 = JR | last4 = Solomon | first4 = MJ | year = 2008 | title = Auditory event perception: the source-perception loop for posture in human gait | url = | journal = Percept Psychophys | volume = 70 | issue = 1| pages = 13–29 | doi = 10.3758/PP.70.1.13 | pmid = 18306957 }}</ref> estimate the volume of a container from the sound of liquid being poured into it, or infer the configuration of clapping hands from the sound produced.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Repp | first1 = BH | year = 1987 | title = The sound of two hands clapping: an exploratory study | url = | journal = J Acoust Soc Am | volume = 81 | issue = 4| pages = 1100–1109 | doi = 10.1121/1.394630 | pmid = 3571727 | bibcode = 1987ASAJ...81.1100R }}</ref>

=== Ecologically based taxonomies ===
Efforts have been also made to develop comprehensive ecologically based taxonomies that would apply to sounds of everyday listening environments<ref>Jenkins JJ (1985) Acoustic information for objects, places and events. In: Warren WH, Shaw RE (eds) Persistence and change. Erlbaum, Hillsdale, N.J. pp 115-138</ref><ref name=":17" /> and integrate them into broader acoustic communication frameworks.<ref name=":19">Truax B &nbsp;(2001) Acoustic Communication, 2nd edn. Ablex Publishing</ref> However, due to the large inherent variability and complexity of everyday environmental sounds, no general all-inclusive taxonomy has been developed. Nevertheless, valuable classifications have emerged, notably distinguishing between the perception of actions/events and objects/materials.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Warren | first1 = WH | last2 = Verbrugge | first2 = RR | year = 1984 | title = Auditory perception of breaking and bouncing events: a case study in ecological acoustics | url = | journal = Journal of Experimental Psychology. Human Perception and Performance | volume = 10 | issue = 5| pages = 704–12 | doi = 10.1037/0096-1523.10.5.704 | pmid = 6238128 }}</ref><ref name=":17" /><ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Lemaitre | first1 = G | last2 = Pyles | first2 = JA | last3 = Halpern | first3 = AR | last4 = Navolio | first4 = N | last5 = Lehet | first5 = M | last6 = Heller | first6 = HM | year = 2018 | title = Who's that knocking at my door? Neural bases of sound source identification | journal = Cerebral Cortex | volume = 28 | issue = 3| pages = 805–818 | doi = 10.1093/cercor/bhw397 | pmid = 28052922 }}</ref> &nbsp;Experimental approaches so far have also mostly failed to distinguish among different types of listening experience such as active and focused listening versus background listening when listener is not actively seeking one or more specific sounds by monitors environment as a whole.<ref name=":19" />&nbsp;Furthermore, unlike HAE, previous approaches to the study of auditory perception have made no systematic distinction between natural (biophonic or geophonic) versus mechanically or electronically generated and technophonic sounds.

=== Auditory perception of natural soundscapes, biophony and geophony ===
Two psychoacoustical studies have explored the ability of human listeners to discriminate natural soundscapes in a categorical way.<ref name=":11" /><ref name=":13" /> Consistent with predictions of a modelling study,<ref name=":10" /> these behavioral studies reveal that human listeners are able to discriminate soundscapes recorded by soundscape ecologists in a nature reserve with their ears only. More precisely, these studies showed that naive (untrained) listeners hear changes in habitat (forest, meadow, grassland, chaparral), moment of the day (dawn, dusk, etc.) and season (summer, fall, etc.), although this capacity is not optimal. Another psychoacoustical study based on 200 scenes including natural settings (forests, parks, hiking trails) corroborated the idea that human listeners consistently perceive global attributes of natural soundscapes, that is structural properties (e.g., openness), constancy properties (e.g., transience), or functional properties (e.g., navigability).<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = McMullin | first1 = MA | last2 = Kumar | first2 = R | last3 = Higgins | first3 = NC | last4 = Gygi | first4 = B | last5 = Elhilali | first5 = M | last6 = Snyder | first6 = JS | date = Mar 2024 | title = Preliminary Evidence for Global Properties in Human Listeners During Natural Auditory Scene Perception | url = | journal =Open Mind (Camb) | volume = 8 | issue = | pages = 333–365 | doi = 10.1162/opmi_a_00131 | pmid = 38571530 | pmc = 10990578 }}</ref> These first studies pave the way for further empirical studies aiming to assess the human ability to perceive natural soundscapes and their variations using the sound databases recorded by soundscape ecologists and ecoacousticians.

===Statistical representation in ecological sound perception===
Many natural environments, such as a flock of birds singing in the trees or a swarm of insects chirping in the grass are composed of large numbers of similar sound events. As a result, their properties tend to be fairly stable over time. Such sounds are commonly referred to as "textures", and are thought to be represented in the auditory system with summary statistics.<ref>Saint-Arnaud, N., and Popat, K. (1995). Analysis and synthesis of sound texture. Proceedings of AJCAI Workshop on Computational Auditory Scene Analysis, pp. 293–308.</ref><ref name=":16" /> Specifically, the brain appears to base its decisions about such sounds on a time-averaged statistical representation, and does not retain the temporal acoustic detail of the individual sound features composing the texture.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = McDermott | first1 = J. H. | last2 = Schemitsch | first2 = M. | last3 = Simoncelli | first3 = E. P. | year = 2013 | title = Summary statistics in auditory perception | url = | journal = Nature Neuroscience | volume = 16 | issue = 4| pages = 493–498 | doi = 10.1038/nn.3347 | pmid = 23434915 | pmc = 4143328 }}</ref> This average appears to be computed over a window of several seconds.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = McWalter | first1 = R. | last2 = McDermott | first2 = J. H. | year = 2018 | title = Adaptive and selective time averaging of auditory scenes | url = | journal = Current Biology | volume = 28 | issue = 9| pages = 1405–1418 | doi = 10.1016/j.cub.2018.03.049 | pmid = 29681472 | pmc = 5940576 | bibcode = 2018CBio...28E1405M }}</ref> The averaging process occurs even in the presence of other sounds, and helps us to hear other sounds that occur concurrently with a texture.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Hicks | first1 = J. M. | last2 = McDermott | first2 = J. H. | year = 2024 | title = Noise schemas aid hearing in noise | url = | journal = Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences | volume = 121 | issue = 47| article-number = e2408995121 | doi = 10.1073/pnas.2408995121 | doi-access = free | pmid = 39546566 | pmc = 11588100 | bibcode = 2024PNAS..12108995H }}</ref> The brain even appears to "fill in" the statistical properties of a texture when it is temporarily masked by a concurrent sound.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = McWalter | first1 = R. | last2 = McDermott | first2 = J. H. | year = 2019 | title = Illusory sound texture reveals multi-second statistical completion in auditory scene analysis | url = | journal = Nature Communications | volume = 10 | issue = 1| page = 5096 | doi = 10.1038/s41467-019-12893-0 | pmid = 31704913 | pmc = 6841952 | bibcode = 2019NatCo..10.5096M }}</ref> In contrast, for sounds that are more temporally sparse, such as one bird singing, or a branch breaking, the auditory system does retain the temporal acoustic detail. It appears the auditory system employs two modes when listening to ecological sounds, one that retains the acoustic detail when the sounds are temporally sparse, and the other that averages the acoustic properties over time when the sounds are temporally dense. Although these two modes of hearing have been explored for individual sound sources and for superpositions of a texture with other sounds, we know less about how the auditory system operates when we hear mixtures of ecological sounds. This latter question is an active area of research in human auditory ecology.

== Developmental aspects ==
If human perceptual systems have evolved to efficiently encode environmental signals, as suggested by the efficient [[neural coding]] hypothesis,<ref>{{cite book | last1=Barlow | first1=H. B. | title=Sensory Communication | chapter=Possible Principles Underlying the Transformations of Sensory Messages | date=2012 | pages=216–234 | doi=10.7551/mitpress/9780262518420.003.0013 | isbn=978-0-262-51842-0 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Simoncelli | first1 = E. P. | last2 = Olshausen | first2 = B. A. | year = 2001 | title = Natural image statistics and neural representation | url = | journal = Annual Review of Neuroscience | volume = 24 | issue = | pages = 1193–1216 | doi = 10.1146/annurev.neuro.24.1.1193 | pmid = 11520932 }}</ref> then these mechanisms may be evolutionarily ancestral, and as such may emerge early in ontogenetic development.<ref>{{cite journal | last1=Gervain | first1=Judit | last2=Geffen | first2=Maria N. | title=Efficient Neural Coding in Auditory and Speech Perception | journal=Trends in Neurosciences | date=2019 | volume=42 | issue=1 | pages=56–65 | doi=10.1016/j.tins.2018.09.004 | pmid=30297085 | pmc=6542557 }}</ref> Few studies to date have explicitly tested this hypothesis for natural sounds, and in general few studies have looked at how young children perceive environmental sounds other than speech and music.

One series of studies.<ref name=":20">{{cite journal | last1 = Geffen | first1 = M. N. | last2 = Gervain | first2 = J. | last3 = Werker | first3 = J. F. | last4 = Magnasco | first4 = M. O. | year = 2011 | title = Auditory perception of self-similarity in water sounds | url = | journal = Front. Integr. Neurosci. | volume = 5 | issue = | page = 15 | doi = 10.3389/fnint.2011.00015 | pmid = 21617734 | pmc = 3095814 | doi-access = free }}</ref><ref name=":21">{{cite journal | last1 = Gervain | first1 = J. | last2 = Werker | first2 = J. F. | last3 = Black | first3 = A. | last4 = Geffen | first4 = M. N. | year = 2016 | title = The neural correlates of processing scale-invariant environmental sounds at birth | url = | journal = NeuroImage | volume = 133 | issue = | pages = 144–150 | doi = 10.1016/j.neuroimage.2016.03.001 | pmid = 26956907 }}</ref> investigated adults and young infants' perception of water sounds. According to the efficient neural coding hypothesis, perceptual systems need to extract the statistical structure of environmental stimuli in order to achieve an information theoretical optimum, i.e. encoding the greatest amount of information at the lowest cost. One common feature of the statistical structure of many natural stimuli is scale-invariance, the property of exhibiting the same statistical structure at different spatial or temporal scales. The studies conducted with water sounds tested whether sounds produced by a generative model of gamma tone chirps obeying scale-invariance were perceived as instances of natural water sounds, while those having a variable-scale structure were not. Indeed, adults rated a wide range of scale-invariant, but not variable-scale, sounds as natural recordings of brooks and streams, and qualitatively described them as various forms of water.<ref name=":20" /> Five-month-old infants also showed categorical discrimination between scale-invariant and variable-scale sounds,<ref name=":12" /> and the newborn brain just 1–3 days after birth responded differentially to scale-invariant and variable-scale water sounds in the left inferior frontal and temporal areas<ref name=":21" />

In addition, many animal species communicate with auditory signals, and two groups have received particular attention in studies of children's perceptual sensitivities: primates, our closest phylogenetic relatives, and birds, some of which produce complex, acoustically rich vocalizations. Research has shown that from birth to about 3–4 months, infants do not exhibit selective processing for human speech over non-human primate vocalizations, although they maintain preferences for biological over artificial sounds. By 4–6 months, experience sharpens preferences toward speech, with neural responses diverging for human and primate vocalizations.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Perszyk | first1 = D. R. | last2 = Waxman | first2 = S. R. | year = 2016 | title = Listening to the calls of the wild: the role of experience in linking language and cognition in young infants | url = | journal = Cognition | volume = 153 | issue = | pages = 175–181 | doi = 10.1016/j.cognition.2016.05.004 | pmid = 27209387 | pmc = 5134735 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Vouloumanos | first1 = A. | last2 = Hauser | first2 = M. D. | last3 = Werker | first3 = J. F. | last4 = Martin | first4 = A. | year = 2010 | title = The tuning of human neonates' preference for speech | url = | journal =Child Dev | volume = 81 | issue = 2| pages = 517–527 | doi = 10.1111/j.1467-8624.2009.01412.x | pmid = 20438457 }}</ref> Infants also show sensitivity to bird songs, with preferences for low-frequency seabird calls over higher-frequency garden bird songs, a pattern observed across different cultural contexts and possibly reflecting universal predispositions modulated by local exposure.<ref>{{cite journal | last1=Lange-Küttner | first1=Christiane | title=Discrimination of sea-bird sounds vs. Garden-bird songs: Do Scottish and German-Saxon infants show the same preferential looking behaviour as adults? | journal=European Journal of Developmental Psychology | date=2010 | volume=7 | issue=5 | pages=578–602 | doi=10.1080/17405620902937531 }}</ref> Moreover, comparisons between bird song and speech suggest that infants prefer naturally produced sounds, human or non-human, over temporally distorted speech.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Santolin | first1 = C. | last2 = Russo | first2 = S. | last3 = Calignano | first3 = G. | last4 = Saffran | first4 = J. R. | last5 = Valenza | first5 = E. | year = 2019 | title = The role of prosody in infants' preference for speech: a comparison between speech and birdsong | url = | journal = Infancy | volume = 24 | issue = 5| pages = 827–833 | doi = 10.1111/infa.12295 | pmid = 32677275 }}</ref>

These results suggest that the perception of natural sounds may indeed be efficient early on in human development, and pave the way for further studies of this hypothesis involving a much greater variety of natural sounds and soundscapes. Indeed, determining whether children can discriminate between natural sounds, may reveal critical periods for tuning to environmental auditory features, analogous to those proposed for speech and music perception.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Polver | first1 = S. | last2 = Miller-Viacava | first2 = N. | last3 = Fraticelli | first3 = M. | last4 = Gervain | first4 = J. | last5 = Lorenzi | first5 = C. | year = 2024 | title = Developmental origins of natural sound perception | url = | journal = Frontiers in Psychology | volume = 15 | issue = | article-number = 1474961 | doi = 10.3389/fpsyg.2024.1474961 | pmid = 39726626 | pmc = 11669913 | doi-access = free }}</ref>

== Emotional aspects and health benefits ==

=== Psychological studies ===
Emotion perception of environmental sounds can be described within the dimensional view of affect, specifically along a combination of several continua.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Russell | first1 = J.A. | year = 1980 | title = A circumplex model of affect | url = | journal = Journal of Personality and Social Psychology | volume = 39 | issue = 6| pages = 1161–1178 | doi = 10.1037/h0077714 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Watson | first1 = C.S. | last2 = Foyle | first2 = D.C. | year = 1985 | title = Central factors in the discrimination and identification of complex sounds | url = | journal = The Journal of the Acoustical Society of America | volume = 78 | issue = 1 Pt 2| pages = 375–380 | doi = 10.1121/1.392450 | pmid = 4031244 | bibcode = 1985ASAJ...78..375W }}</ref> Two dimensions often dominate, specifically valence (unpleasant to pleasant) and arousal (calming to exciting),<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Russell | first1 = J.A. | last2 = Mehrabian | first2 = A. | year = 1977 | title = Evidence for a three-factor theory of emotions | url = | journal = Journal of Research in Personality | volume = 11 | issue = 3| pages = 273–294 | doi = 10.1016/0092-6566(77)90037-X }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Bradley | first1 = M.M. | last2 = Lang | first2 = P.J. | year = 1994 | title = Measuring emotion: the self-assessment manikin and the semantic differential | url = | journal = Journal of Behavior Therapy and Experimental Psychiatry | volume = 25 | issue = 1| pages = 49–59 | doi = 10.1016/0005-7916(94)90063-9 | pmid = 7962581 }}</ref> Although emotions could be high on either nor neither dimension, sounds that elicit emotions near the valence extremes (e.g., very pleasant or very unpleasant), also tend to be high in the arousal dimension (e.g., exciting or activating). The valence dimension reflects the motivational theory of emotion, where valence of an emotion supports a person's motivation.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Bradley | first1 = M.M. | last2 = Lang | first2 = P.J. | year = 2000 | title = Affective reactions to acoustic stimuli | url = | journal = Psychophysiology | volume = 37 | issue = 2| pages = 204–215 | doi = 10.1111/1469-8986.3720204 | pmid = 10731770 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Andringa | first1 = T.C. | last2 = Lanser | first2 = J.J.L. | year = 2013 | title = How pleasant sounds promote and annoying sounds impede health: A cognitive approach | url = | journal = International Journal of Environmental Research and Public Health | volume = 10 | issue = 4| pages = 1439–1461 | doi = 10.3390/ijerph10041439 | pmid = 23567255 | pmc = 3709327 | doi-access = free | bibcode = 2013IJERP..10.1439A }}</ref> Unpleasant emotions (e.g., in response to a lion) facilitate attention and inspire action.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Baumeister | first1 = R.F. | display-authors = etal | year = 2001 | title = Bad is stronger than good | url = | journal = Review of General Psychology | volume = 5 | issue = 4| pages = 323–370 | doi = 10.1037/1089-2680.5.4.323 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Kensinger | first1 = E.A. | year = 2009 | title = Remembering the details: Effects of emotion. | url = | journal = Emotion Review | volume = 1 | issue = 2| pages = 99–113 | doi = 10.1177/1754073908100432 | pmid = 19421427 | pmc = 2676782 }}</ref> Pleasant emotions (e.g., in response to birdsong) encourage approach behavior and support well-being, such as through stress recovery<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Sandstrom | first1 = G.M. | last2 = Russo | first2 = F.A. | year = 2010 | title = Music hath charms: the effects of valence and arousal on recovery following an acute stressor | url = | journal = Music and Medicine | volume = 2 | issue = 3| pages = 137–143 | doi = 10.1177/1943862110371486 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Alvarsson | first1 = J.J. | last2 = Wiens | first2 = S. | last3 = Nilsson | first3 = M.E. | year = 2010 | title = Stress recovery during exposure to nature sound and environmental noise | url = | journal = International Journal of Environmental Research and Public Health | volume = 7 | issue = 3| pages = 1036–1046 | doi = 10.3390/ijerph7031036 | pmid = 20617017 | pmc = 2872309 | doi-access = free }}</ref>&nbsp;or creative thinking.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Fredrickson | first1 = B.L. | year = 2001 | title = The role of positive emotions in positive psychology: The broaden-and-build theory of positive emotions | url = | journal = American Psychologist | volume = 56 | issue = 3| pages = 218–226 | doi = 10.1037/0003-066X.56.3.218 | pmid = 11315248 | pmc = 3122271 }}</ref> Thus, both pleasant and unpleasant emotions serve important functions. Adults with permanent, sensorineural hearing loss do not report a full range of emotional responses to non-speech sounds; their range of ratings of valence is less extreme (less pleasant, less unpleasant) compared to their peers with normal hearing, even when they are similarly aged.<ref name=":22">{{cite journal | last1 = Picou | first1 = E.M. | year = 2016 | title = How hearing loss and age affect emotional responses to nonspeech sounds | url = | journal = Journal of Speech, Language, and Hearing Research | volume = 59 | issue = 5| pages = 1233–1246 | doi = 10.1044/2016_JSLHR-H-15-0231 | pmid = 27768178 }}</ref><ref name=":23">{{cite journal | last1 = Picou | first1 = E.M. | display-authors = etal | year = 2021 | title = Effects of increasing the overall level or fitting hearing aids on emotional responses to sounds | url = | journal = Trends in Hearing | volume = 25 | issue = | pages = 1–13 | article-number = 23312165211049938 | doi = 10.1177/23312165211049938 | pmid = 34866509 | pmc = 8825634 }}</ref>&nbsp;This is true for natural and manmade sounds.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Marcrum | first1 = S.C. | last2 = Rakita | first2 = L. | last3 = Picou | first3 = E.M. | year = | title = Emotional responses to sound for people with hearing loss: Effects of sound category | url = | journal = Emotion | volume =  | issue = | page =  }}</ref>

=== Soundscape ecology and ecoacoustics ===
Exposure to nature provides a variety of health benefits.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Frumkin | first1 = H. | display-authors = etal | year = 2017 | title = "Nature contact and human health " a research agenda | url = | journal = Environmental Health Perspectives | volume = 125 | issue = 7| article-number = 075001 | doi = 10.1289/EHP1663 | doi-broken-date = 5 February 2026 | pmid = 28796634 | pmc = 5744722 | bibcode = 2017EnvHP.125g5001F }}</ref> Soundscapes in particular provide crucial information, where sounds enable most species, including humans, to surveil their surroundings.<ref name=":14" /><ref>{{cite journal | last1=Francis | first1=Clinton D. | last2=Newman | first2=Peter | last3=Taff | first3=B. Derrick | last4=White | first4=Crow | last5=Monz | first5=Christopher A. | last6=Levenhagen | first6=Mitchell | last7=Petrelli | first7=Alissa R. | last8=Abbott | first8=Lauren C. | last9=Newton | first9=Jennifer | last10=Burson | first10=Shan | last11=Cooper | first11=Caren B. | last12=Fristrup | first12=Kurt M. | last13=McClure | first13=Christopher J.W. | last14=Mennitt | first14=Daniel | last15=Giamellaro | first15=Michael | last16=Barber | first16=Jesse R. | title=Acoustic environments matter: Synergistic benefits to humans and ecological communities | journal=Journal of Environmental Management | date=2017 | volume=203 | issue=Pt 1 | pages=245–254 | doi=10.1016/j.jenvman.2017.07.041 | pmid=28783021 | bibcode=2017JEnvM.203..245F }}</ref> From an evolutionary perspective,<ref>Katcher, A. and G. Wilkins, ''Dialogue with animals: its nature and culture'', in ''The Biophilia Hypothesis'', S.R. Kellert and E.O. Wilson, Editors. 1993, Island Press: Washington, DC</ref> a soundscape that is full of natural sounds, can be an indicator of an environment rich in resources needed for survival. Thus, a natural acoustic environment stimulates the [[parasympathetic nervous system]], allowing mental recuperation and a reduction in stress-related behaviour. Two psychological theories explain the mechanistic basis of the restorative effects of exposure to natural soundscapes: [[Attention restoration theory|Attention Restoration Theory]], the ability of nature to replenish attention<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Kaplan | first1 = S | year = 1995 | title = The restorative benefits of nature: toward an integrative framework | url = | journal = Journal of Environmental Psychology | volume = 15 | issue = 3| pages = 169–182 | doi = 10.1016/0272-4944(95)90001-2 }}</ref> and Stress Recovery theory, where nature is less arousing than fatigue-inducing urban environments, leading to recovery from stress.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Ulrich | first1 = R.S. | display-authors = etal | year = 1991 | title = Stress recovery during exposure to natural and urban environments | url = | journal = Journal of Environmental Psychology | volume = 11 | issue = 3| pages = 201–230 | doi = 10.1016/S0272-4944(05)80184-7 | bibcode = 1991JEPsy..11..201U }}</ref> A synthesis of studies examining the evidence of health benefits of natural soundscapes revealed decreased stress and annoyance and improved health and positive affective outcomes.<ref name=":24">{{cite journal | last1 = Buxton | first1 = R.T. | display-authors = etal | year = 2021 | title = A synthesis of health benefits of natural sounds and their distribution in national parks | url = | journal = Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences | volume = 118 | issue = 14| article-number = e2013097118 | doi = 10.1073/pnas.2013097118 | pmid = 33753555 | pmc = 8040792 | bibcode = 2021PNAS..11813097B | doi-access = free }}</ref> Examples of beneficial outcomes of listening to natural sounds included decreased pain, lower stress, improved mood, and enhanced cognitive performance<ref name=":24" />

=== Soundscape studies ===
Soundscape studies play an important role within the framework of HAE, focusing on human sensory and emotional auditory processing.<ref>Schulte-Fortkamp, B., Fiebig, A., Sisneros, J. A., Popper, A. N., & Fay, R. R. (Eds.). (2023). ''Soundscapes: Humans and their acoustic environment''. Springer</ref><ref>Kang, J., & Schulte-Fortkamp, B. (Eds.). (2016). ''Soundscape and the built environment''(Vol. 525). Boca Raton, FL, USA: CRC press</ref> Defined by ISO 12913 as the acoustic environment perceived by humans within a contextual framework,<ref name=":25">International Organization for Standardization. (2014). ''ISO 12913-1:2014 Acoustics&nbsp;— Soundscape&nbsp;— Part 1: Definition and conceptual framework.'' Geneva: ISO</ref> soundscape studies diverge from traditional environmental noise research by emphasizing positive health and perceptual outcomes,<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Aletta | first1 = F. | last2 = Oberman | first2 = T. | last3 = Kang | first3 = J. | year = 2018 | title = Associations between positive health-related effects and soundscapes perceptual constructs: A systematic review | url = | journal = International Journal of Environmental Research and Public Health | volume = 15 | issue = 11| page = 2392 | doi = 10.3390/ijerph15112392 | pmid = 30380601 | doi-access = free | pmc = 6266166 | bibcode = 2018IJERP..15.2392A }}</ref> particularly regarding the (measurable) restorative properties of natural sounds.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Ratcliffe | first1 = E | year = 2021 | title = Sound and soundscape in restorative natural environments: A narrative literature review | url = | journal = Frontiers in Psychology | volume = 12 | issue = | article-number = 570563 | doi = 10.3389/fpsyg.2021.570563 | pmid = 33981262 | pmc = 8107214 | doi-access = free }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Payne | first1 = S. R. | year = 2013 | title = The production of a perceived restorativeness soundscape scale | url = | journal = Applied Acoustics | volume = 74 | issue = 2| pages = 255–263 | doi = 10.1016/j.apacoust.2011.11.005 | bibcode = 2013ApAc...74..255P }}</ref> Additionally, there are important soundscape studies within spatial design such as Landscape Architecture, urban planning etc.<ref>Hedfors, Per (2003). Site soundscapes: landscape architecture in the light of sound. Diss. Uppsala: SLU. http://epsilon.slu.se/a407.pdf
</ref><ref>Cerwén, Gunnar (2017). Sound in landscape architecture: a soundscape approach to noise. Diss. Alnarp: SLU http://urn.kb.se/resolve?urn=urn:nbn:se:slu:epsilon-e-4380</ref> Contrary to assumptions within HAE, soundscape studies reveal cultural variations in individuals' and communities' responses to natural sounds.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Aletta | first1 = F. | last2 = Oberman | first2 = T. | last3 = Mitchell | first3 = A. | last4 = Erfanian | first4 = M. | last5 = Kang | first5 = J. | year = 2023 | title = Soundscape experience of public spaces in different world regions: A comparison between the European and Chinese contexts via a large-scale on-site survey | url = | journal = The Journal of the Acoustical Society of America | volume = 154 | issue = 3| pages = 1710–1734 | doi = 10.1121/10.0020842 | pmid = 37712752 | bibcode = 2023ASAJ..154.1710A | doi-access = free }}</ref> The ISO 12913 series, comprising theoretical frameworks,<ref name=":25" /> data collection<ref>International Organization for Standardization. (2018). ''ISO/TS 12913-2:2018 Acoustics&nbsp;— Soundscape&nbsp;— Part 2: Data collection and reporting requirements.'' Geneva: ISO</ref> and data analysis methods, serves as a cornerstone in soundscape literature. Advancements in the field include innovative methods for visualizing and analyzing quantitative soundscape data,<ref>International Organization for Standardization. (2019). ''ISO/TS 12913-3:2019 Acoustics&nbsp;— Soundscape&nbsp;— Part 3: Data analysis.''Geneva: ISO</ref> alongside prediction models that simulate human perceptions of present and future/hypothetical acoustic environments.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Mitchell | first1 = A. | last2 = Oberman | first2 = T. | last3 = Aletta | first3 = F. | last4 = Kachlicka | first4 = M. | last5 = Lionello | first5 = M. | last6 = Erfanian | first6 = M. | last7 = Kang | first7 = J. | year = 2021 | title = Investigating urban soundscapes of the COVID-19 lockdown: A predictive soundscape modeling approach | url = | journal = The Journal of the Acoustical Society of America | volume = 150 | issue = 6| pages = 4474–4488 | doi = 10.1121/10.0008928 | pmid = 34972283 | pmc = 8730329 | bibcode = 2021ASAJ..150.4474M }}</ref> These models utilize objective metrics as predictors and subjective metrics as descriptors,<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Aletta | first1 = F. | last2 = Kang | first2 = J. | last3 = Axelsson | first3 = Ö. | year = 2016 | title = Soundscape descriptors and a conceptual framework for developing predictive soundscape models | url = | journal = Landscape and Urban Planning | volume = 149 | issue = | pages = 65–74 | doi = 10.1016/j.landurbplan.2016.02.001 | bibcode = 2016LUrbP.149...65A }}</ref> enabling a deeper understanding of how humans experience acoustic environments within context and driving progress in understanding and optimizing auditory experiences in natural habitats.

== Audiological aspects ==

=== Effects of aging and hearing loss ===
Older adults with normal hearing or with mild hearing losses appear to maintain generally robust source recognition of environmental sounds in quiet.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Ballas | first1 = JA | last2 = Barnes | first2 = ME | year = 1988 | title = Everyday sound perception and aging | url = | journal = Proceedings of the Human Factors Society Annual Meeting | volume = 32 | issue = 3| pages = 194–197 | doi = 10.1177/154193128803200305 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Harris | first1 = MS | last2 = Boyce | first2 = L | last3 = Pisoni | first3 = DB | last4 = Shafiro | first4 = V | last5 = Moberly | first5 = AC | year = 2017 | title = The relationship between environmental sound awareness and speech recognition skills in experienced cochlear implant users | url = | journal = Otology & Neurotology | volume = 38 | issue = 9| pages = e308–e314 | doi = 10.1097/MAO.0000000000001514 | pmid = 28731964 | pmc = 6205294 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | last1=Shafiro | first1=Valeriy | last2=Hebb | first2=Megan | last3=Walker | first3=Chad | last4=Oh | first4=Jasper | last5=Hsiao | first5=Ying | last6=Brown | first6=Kelly | last7=Sheft | first7=Stanley | last8=Li | first8=Yan | last9=Vasil | first9=Kara | last10=Moberly | first10=Aaron C. | title=Development of the Basic Auditory Skills Evaluation Battery for Online Testing of Cochlear Implant Listeners | journal=American Journal of Audiology | date=2020 | volume=29 | issue=3S | pages=577–590 | doi=10.1044/2020_AJA-19-00083 | pmid=32946250 | pmc=7838832 }}</ref> However, when tested under more challenging conditions which closer approximate everyday listening environments, older adults with and without hearing loss require greater [[signal-to-noise ratio]] (SNR) to identify sounds in scenes.<ref>Dick F, Krishna S, Leech R, Saygin AP (2016). Environmental sounds. In: Hickok G, Small SL (ed) Neurobiology of language. Academic Press, pp 1121-1138   {{doi|10.1523/JNEUROSCI.0419-05.2005}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Gygi | first1 = B | last2 = Shafiro | first2 = V | year = 2013 | title = Auditory and cognitive effects of aging on perception of environmental sounds in natural auditory scenes [published correction appears in J Speech Lang Hear Res. 2013 Dec;56(6) 1934] | journal = J Speech Lang Hear Res | volume = 56 | issue = 5| pages = 1373–1388 | doi = 10.1044/1092-4388(2013/12-0283) | pmid = 23926291 | pmc = 3839956 }}</ref> Middle-aged and older adults with normal hearing and those with some degree of sensorineural hearing loss also perform poorer than younger adults in tasks which involve perception of multiple environmental sounds.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Shafiro | first1 = V | last2 = Sheft | first2 = S | last3 = Norris | first3 = M | last4 = Spanos | first4 = G | last5 = Radasevich | first5 = K | last6 = Formsma | first6 = P | display-authors = etal | year = 2016 | title = Toward a Nonspeech Test of Auditory Cognition: Semantic Context Effects in Environmental Sound Identification in Adults of Varying Age and Hearing Abilities | journal = PLOS ONE | volume = 11 | issue = 11| article-number = e0167030 | doi = 10.1371/journal.pone.0167030 | pmid = 27893791 | pmc = 5125666 | bibcode = 2016PLoSO..1167030S | doi-access = free }}</ref> Furthermore, irrespective of age and severity of hearing loss, people with hearing loss exhibit great difficulties discriminating natural soundscapes that vary systematically in terms of place (forest, meadow, grassland, chaparral), moment of the day (dawn, midday, dusk and night) and season (autumn, winter, spring and summer)<ref name=":13" />

=== Effects of conventional hearing aids ===
The primary complaint of people with hearing impairment is difficulty to understand speech in noise.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Woods | first1 = D. L. | last2 = Arbogast | first2 = T. | last3 = Doss | first3 = Z. | last4 = Younus | first4 = M. | last5 = Herron | first5 = T.J. | last6 = Yund | first6 = W.E. | year = 2015 | title = Aided and unaided speech perception by older hearing impaired listeners | url = | journal = PLOS ONE | volume = 10 | issue = 3| article-number = e0114922 | doi = 10.1371/journal.pone.0114922 | pmid = 25730423 | pmc = 4346396 | bibcode = 2015PLoSO..1014922W | doi-access = free }}</ref> As such, substantial knowledge has been acquired about mechanisms behind [[speech perception]] (2) and several speech-in-noise testing tools have been developed.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Ellis | first1 = RJ | last2 = Molander | first2 = P | last3 = Rönnberg | first3 = J | last4 = Lyxell | first4 = B | last5 = Andersson | first5 = G | last6 = Lunner | first6 = T | year = 2016 | title = Predicting Speech-in-Noise Recognition From Performance on the Trail Making Test: Results From a Large-Scale Internet Study | url = | journal = Ear Hear | volume = 37 | issue = 1| pages = 73–9 | doi = 10.1097/AUD.0000000000000218 | pmid = 26317162 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Kuk | first1 = F | last2 = Slugocki | first2 = C | last3 = Korhonen | first3 = P | year = 2020 | title = Using the Repeat-Recall Test to Examine Factors Affecting Context Use | url = | journal = J Am Acad Audiol | volume = 31 | issue = 10| pages = 771–780 | doi = 10.1055/s-0040-1719136 | pmid = 33588513 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | last1=Zaar | first1=Johannes | last2=Simonsen | first2=Lisbeth Birkelund | last3=Sanchez-Lopez | first3=Raul | last4=Laugesen | first4=Søren | title=The Audible Contrast Threshold (ACT) test: A clinical spectro-temporal modulation detection test | journal=Hearing Research | date=2024 | volume=453 | article-number=109103 | doi=10.1016/j.heares.2024.109103 |medrxiv=10.1101/2023.10.12.23296977 | pmid=39243488 | doi-access=free }}</ref> People with hearing impairment can benefit from the use of hearing aids.<ref>World Health Organization. (2015). Deafness and hearing loss Fact sheet N°300</ref> Research in auditory reality of [[hearing aid]] users has shown that people spend approximately 31% of time in situations involving speech communication, whereas they spend about 45% of time in situations where they are monitoring surroundings or passive listening.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Smeds | first1 = K. | last2 = Gotowiec | first2 = S. | last3 = Wolters | first3 = F. | last4 = Herrlin | first4 = P. | last5 = Larsson | first5 = J. | last6 = Dahlquist | first6 = M. | year = 2020 | title = Selecting scenarios for hearing-related laboratory testing | url = | journal =Ear Hear | volume = 41 | issue = Suppl 1| pages = 20S–30S | doi = 10.1097/AUD.0000000000000930 | pmid = 33105256 | doi-access = free }}</ref> It has been proposed that HAE should not be limited to the case of urban settings and communication but should be extended to perception of wild or rural soundscapes.<ref name=":5" /> The ability to experience sounds of nature can lead to feeling less pain, lower stress, enhanced mood and improved cognitive performance.<ref name=":24" /> People who use hearing aids report positive listening experiences that involve not only communication, but also perception of environmental and nature sounds (e.g., "the forest sounds completely different," "nice to hear the wind howling," "can hear traffic and the rain," "heard rustling leaves and rustling of the trees," "can hear the sparrow singing.").<ref>{{cite journal | pmid = 37005863 | doi=10.1080/14992027.2023.2190006 | volume=63 | title=Focusing on positive listening experiences improves hearing aid outcomes in experienced hearing aid users | year=2024 | journal=Int J Audiol | pages=420–430  | last1 = Lelic | first1 = D | last2 = Parker | first2 = D | last3 = Herrlin | first3 = P | last4 = Wolters | first4 = F | last5 = Smeds | first5 = K | issue=6 | doi-access = free }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Lelic | first1 = D. | last2 = Nielsen | first2 = L.L.A. | last3 = Pedersen | first3 = A.K. | last4 = Neher | first4 = T. | year = 2024 | title = Focusing on Positive Listening Experiences Improves Speech Intelligibility in Experienced Hearing Aid Users | url = | journal = Trends in Hearing | volume =  28| issue = | article-number = 23312165241246616 | doi = 10.1177/23312165241246616 | pmid = 38656770 | pmc = 11044800 }}</ref>

=== Effects of cochlear implants ===
First time users of sensory aids, hearing aids and cochlear implants often report new or renewed ability to recognize various natural, machine-produced or electronic sounds in their environments.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Lelic | first1 = D. | last2 = Wolters | first2 = F. | last3 = Herrlin | first3 = P. | last4 = Smeds | first4 = K. | year = 2022 | title = Assessment of Hearing-Related Lifestyle Based on the Common Sound Scenarios Framework | url = | journal = American Journal of Audiology | volume = 31 | issue = 4| pages = 1299–1311 | doi = 10.1044/2022_AJA-22-00079 | pmid = 36367531 | doi-access = free }}</ref><ref name=":18" /><ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Shafiro | first1 = Valeriy PhD | last2 = Harris | first2 = Michael S. MD | last3 = Moberly | first3 = Aaron C. MD. | year = 2017| title = Daily Sound Awareness of CI Users | url = | journal = The Hearing Journal | volume = 70 | issue = 5| pages = 8–9 | doi = 10.1097/01.HJ.0000516774.15669.a9 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Lelic | first1 = D | last2 = Picou | first2 = E | last3 = Shafiro | first3 = V | last4 = Lorenzi | first4 = C | date = Nov 2024 | title = Sounds of Nature and Hearing Loss: A Call to Action | url = | journal =Ear Hear | volume =  46| issue = 2| pages =  298–304| doi = 10.1097/AUD.0000000000001601 | pmid = 39506198 | pmc = 11825482 }}</ref>&nbsp;Improvement in environmental sound perception is frequently cited by adults with hearing loss as an anticipated benefit of sensory aids.<ref>{{cite journal | last1=Tyler | first1=R. S. | last2=Kelsay | first2=D. | title=Advantages and disadvantages reported by some of the better cochlear-implant patients | journal=The American Journal of Otology | date=1990 | volume=11 | issue=4 | pages=282–289 | pmid=2399950 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | last1=Zhao | first1=F. | last2=Stephens | first2=S.D.G. | last3=Sim | first3=S. W. | last4=Meredith | first4=R. | title=The use of qualitative questionnaires in patients having and being considered for cochlear implants | journal=Clinical Otolaryngology and Allied Sciences | date=1997 | volume=22 | issue=3 | pages=254–259 | doi=10.1046/j.1365-2273.1997.00036.x | pmid=9222632 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | last1=Parkinson | first1=Aaron J. | last2=Parkinson | first2=Wendy S. | last3=Tyler | first3=Richard S. | last4=Lowder | first4=Mary W. | last5=Gantz | first5=Bruce J. | title=Speech Perception Performance in Experienced Cochlear-Implant Patients Receiving the SPEAK Processing Strategy in the Nucleus Spectra-22 Cochlear Implant | journal=Journal of Speech, Language, and Hearing Research | date=1998 | volume=41 | issue=5 | pages=1073–1087 | doi=10.1044/jslhr.4105.1073  | pmid=9771630 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | last1=McRackan | first1=Theodore R. | last2=Velozo | first2=Craig A. | last3=Holcomb | first3=Meredith A. | last4=Camposeo | first4=Elizabeth L. | last5=Hatch | first5=Jonathan L. | last6=Meyer | first6=Ted A. | last7=Lambert | first7=Paul R. | last8=Melvin | first8=Cathy L. | last9=Dubno | first9=Judy R. | title=Use of Adult Patient Focus Groups to Develop the Initial Item Bank for a Cochlear Implant Quality-of-Life Instrument | journal=JAMA Otolaryngology–Head & Neck Surgery | date=2017 | volume=143 | issue=10 | pages=975–982 | doi=10.1001/jamaoto.2017.1182 | pmid=28772297 | pmc=5710256 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | last1 = McRackan | first1 = T.R. | last2 = Hand | first2 = B.N. | last3 = Velozo | first3 = C.A. | last4 = Dubno | first4 = J.R. | year = 2019 | title = Development of the cochlear implant quality of life item bank. Cochlear Implant Quality of Life Development Consortium | url = | journal = Ear & Hearing | volume = 40 | issue = 4| pages = 1016–1024 | doi = 10.1097/AUD.0000000000000684 | pmid = 30531259 | pmc = 6749825 }}</ref> Nevertheless, when explicitly tested, the ability of [[cochlear implant]] users to identify common environmental sounds shows considerable decrement compared to normal hearing peers.<ref name=":26">{{cite journal | last1 = Shafiro | first1 = V | last2 = Luzum | first2 = N | last3 = Moberly | first3 = AC | last4 = Harris | first4 = MS | date = Jan 2022 | title = Perception of Environmental Sounds in Cochlear Implant Users: A Systematic Review | journal = Front Neurosci | volume = 15 | issue = | article-number = 788899 | doi = 10.3389/fnins.2021.788899 | doi-access = free | pmid = 35082595 | pmc = 8785216 }}</ref> Limited current research, further indicates no significant improvement in environmental sound perception following implantation.<ref>{{cite journal | last1=McMahon | first1=Kevin R. | last2=Moberly | first2=Aaron C. | last3=Shafiro | first3=Valeriy | last4=Harris | first4=Michael S. | title=Environmental Sound Awareness in Experienced Cochlear Implant Users and Cochlear Implant Candidates | journal=Otology & Neurotology | date=2018 | volume=39 | issue=10 | pages=e964–e971 | doi=10.1097/MAO.0000000000002006 | pmid=30252797 | pmc=6242748 }}</ref><ref name=":26" /><ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Looi | first1 = V. | last2 = Arnephy | first2 = J. | year = 2010 | title = Environmental sound perception of cochlear implant users | url = | journal =  Cochlear Implants International| volume = 11 | issue = 4| pages = 203–227 | doi = 10.1002/cii.428 | pmid = 19882594 }}</ref> Given the ubiquity of nonlinguistic environmental sounds as well as their recognized importance for maintaining personal safety, well-being and awareness of their surroundings.<ref name=":17"/><ref>Ramsdell D.A. (1978). The psychology of the hard-of-hearing and the deafened adult. In H. Davis & S.R. Silverman (Eds.), Hearing and deafness (pp. 499–510). New York: Holt, Rinehart and Winston</ref><ref>{{cite journal | title=Cochlear implants in adults and children | journal=NIH Consensus Statement | date=1995 | volume=13 | issue=2 | pages=1–30 | pmid=8595436 }}</ref> environmental sound perception appears to be a fertile area for theoretical and applied auditory perception research<ref name=":18" />

=== Effects of hearing loss and hearing aids on emotional processing ===
Adults with permanent, sensorineural hearing loss do not experience a full range of emotional responses to non-speech sounds; they report emotional responses that are extreme (less pleasant and less unpleasant) than do their similarly aged peers with normal hearing.<ref name=":22" /><ref name=":23" /> The reason for this reduced range of emotional responses is unclear. &nbsp;One explanation is audible bandwidth. Hearing loss in adults commonly causes changes in audible bandwidth, especially reduced audibility of high frequencies, such as those about 2000&nbsp;Hz.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Allen | first1 = P.D. | last2 = Eddins | first2 = D.A. | title = Presbycusis phenotypes form a heterogeneous continuum when ordered by degree and configuration of hearing loss | url = | journal =Hearing Research |year=2010 | volume = 264 | issue = 1| pages = 10–20 | doi = 10.1016/j.heares.2010.02.001 | pmid = 20144701 | pmc = 2868118 }}</ref> However, low- and high-frequency cues (<800 and >2000&nbsp;Hz, respectively) are important for emotional responses to non-speech sounds.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Buono | first1 = G.H. | display-authors = etal | title = Loss of high-or low-frequency audibility can partially explain effects of hearing loss on emotional responses to non-speech sounds | url = | journal =Hearing Research |year=2021 | volume = 401 | issue = | article-number = 108153 | doi = 10.1016/j.heares.2020.108153 | pmid = 33360158 }}</ref> Therefore, improving audibility of high frequency cues, such as with a hearing aid or cochlear implant, would be expected to expand the range of emotional responses to sound. However, even while using the current standard-of-care intervention for permanent hearing loss, including hearing aids, adults demonstrate this reduced range of pleasant responses<ref name=":23" /><ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Tawdrous | first1 = M. | display-authors = etal | year = 2022 | title = Emotional responses to non-speech sounds for hearing-aid and bimodal cochlear-implant listeners | url = | journal = Trends in Hearing | volume = 26 | issue = | pages = 1–17 | article-number = 23312165221083091 | doi = 10.1177/23312165221083091 | pmid = 35435773 | pmc = 9019384 }}</ref> Therefore, other acoustic cues, such as amplitude modulations, might play an important role in emotional processing of non-speech sounds.<ref>Picou, E.M. and W. Martens, ''Modulation Spectral Feature Analysis and Valence of Non-Speech Sounds'', in ''Scientific and Technical Meeting of the American Auditory Society''. 2024: Scottsdale, AZ</ref> Because modern hearing aids have amplitude compression, it is possible that assistive listening devices are reducing the amplitude modulation cues important for emotional responses to sounds.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Picou | first1 = E. | last2 = Martens | first2 = W. | year = 2023 | title = Hearing aid processing affects acoustic features important for emotional responses to sounds | url = | journal = The Journal of the Acoustical Society of America | volume = 154 | issue = 4 supp | pages = A113 | doi = 10.1121/10.0022964 | bibcode = 2023ASAJ..154A.113P }}</ref>

== Auditory awareness of environmental changes ==

=== Contribution of sounsdcape ecology and eco-acoustics ===
Soundscape ecology and ecoacoustics study ecological questions using the analysis of environmental sound.<ref name=":8" /> Because of their unique climate, vegetation and animal communities, ecosystems are associated with environmental sounds showing unique patterns and dynamics.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Lomolino | first1 = M. V. | last2 = Pijanowski | first2 = B. C. | last3 = Gasc | first3 = A. | year = 2015 | title = The silence of biogeography | url = | journal = Journal of Biogeography | volume = 42 | issue = 7| pages = 1187–1196 | doi = 10.1111/jbi.12525 | bibcode = 2015JBiog..42.1187L }}</ref> Changing of those soundscapes have been shown to reflect local disturbances within ecosystems. This is exemplified by the invasive ant ''Wasmannia auropunctata'' affecting the local fauna and therefore silencing the forest of New Caledonia,<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Gasc | first1 = A. | last2 = Anso | first2 = J. | last3 = Sueur | first3 = J. | last4 = Jourdan | first4 = H. | last5 = Desutter-Grandcolas | first5 = L. | year = 2018 | title = Cricket calling communities as an indicator of the invasive ant Wasmannia auropunctata in an insular biodiversity hotspot | url = https://hal.science/hal-01704447| journal = Biological Invasions | volume = 20 | issue = 5| pages = 1099–1111 | doi = 10.1007/s10530-017-1612-0 | bibcode = 2018BiInv..20.1099G }}</ref> the soundscape's diversity showing a flat response in burned area in comparison to unburned area, 3 years after a massive wildfire in the Chiricahua national monument in Arizona<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Gasc | first1 = A. | last2 = Gottesman | first2 = B. L. | last3 = Francomano | first3 = D. | last4 = Jung | first4 = J. | last5 = Durham | first5 = M. | last6 = Mateljak | first6 = J. | last7 = Pijanowski | first7 = B. C. | year = 2018b | title = Soundscapes reveal disturbance impacts: biophonic response to wildfire in the Sonoran Desert Sky Islands | url = https://hal.science/hal-01837168| journal = Landscape Ecology | volume = 33 | issue = 8| pages = 1399–1415 | doi = 10.1007/s10980-018-0675-3 | bibcode = 2018LaEco..33.1399G }}</ref> or the increase in biological sounds in cities during the COVID-19 pandemic.

Ethnographic surveys in human geography highlight the awareness of human beings in the face of these changes.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Sourdril | first1 = A. | last2 = Welch-Devine | first2 = M. | last3 = Andrieu | first3 = E. | last4 = Bélaïdi | first4 = N. | year = 2017 | title = Do April showers bring May flowers? Knowledge and perceptions of local biodiversity influencing understanding of global environmental change. A presentation of the PIAF project | url = | journal = Natures Sciences Sociétés | volume = 25 | issue = | pages = 56–62 | doi = 10.1051/nss/2017009 | doi-access = free }}</ref> Altogether, these findings warrant detailed psychoacoustical investigations aiming to assess the ability of human listeners to hear changes reflecting local disturbances within ecosystems.
